1: YHWH tells Moses to come up with Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders to worship at a distance. The covenant ratification ceremony requires representative witnesses — not all Israel (impossible) but its leadership in ascending tiers: the seventy elders (all Israel's representatives), Aaron and his sons (the priesthood), and Moses (the mediator). The hierarchical structure of the covenant community in its representative leadership anticipates the church's structure: elders, ministers, apostles — all ascending tiers of covenant accountability before God.
2: 'Moses alone shall come near to YHWH; the others shall not come near, and the people shall not come up with him.' The solitary mediator at the summit: Moses alone enters the divine presence at the top. The covenant structure is one of graduated nearness with the mediator at the pinnacle. The NT's counter-movement is striking: Christ is the mediator who has opened the way for all — 'let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith' (Hebrews 10:22). What Moses alone could do, all believers now do through the one greater mediator.
3: Moses came and told the people all the words of YHWH and all the rules. The people's response: 'All the words that YHWH has spoken we will do.' The voice of the covenant community ratifying the covenant terms. This is the covenant oath of Exodus 19:8 repeated with the full covenant code now articulated. The repetition marks the covenant commitment as deliberate and informed — they heard it all and said yes to it all. The NT equivalent is baptismal confession — a public, informed commitment to the covenant terms of the gospel.
4: Moses wrote down all the words of YHWH, rose early, and built an altar with twelve pillars for twelve tribes. The written word as the covenant document and the twelve-tribe altar as the covenant community's representative structure. Writing gives the covenant permanence — it can be read, reviewed, challenged against its original text. The NT covenant document (the NT canon) serves the same function: the written witness to the covenant terms ratified at the cross, accountable to its text.
5: Young men of Israel offered burnt offerings and sacrificed peace offerings to YHWH. The young men (not yet the designated Levites — the Levitical priesthood is not yet established) perform the sacrifice at the covenant ratification. This unusual priestly action by non-designated young men at the covenant founding moment points toward the NT's 'priesthood of all believers' (1 Peter 2:9) — the covenant people as a whole are a 'royal priesthood,' not only the designated few.
6: Moses took half the blood and put it in basins; half he threw against the altar. The blood is divided: half to the altar (YHWH's side) and half to be thrown on the people (Exodus 24:8). The division of the covenant blood between YHWH's altar and the people is the OT's most explicit enactment of the covenant as a bond established in blood — the same blood touching both parties creates the bond between them. The Eucharist's two-party sharing (Christ's body and blood given to the community) is the NT fulfillment of this two-party blood application.
7: Moses took the Book of the Covenant and read it in the hearing of the people, who again said: 'All that YHWH has spoken we will do and we will be obedient.' The covenant document read publicly before the community commits — covenant ratification requires informed public consent. The reading of the covenant aloud to the assembled community becomes a recurring covenant-renewal pattern (Joshua 8:34–35; Nehemiah 8:1–8; Luke 4:16–21 — Jesus reading the scroll in the synagogue and declaring it fulfilled in their hearing).
8: 'Moses took the blood and threw it on the people and said: Behold the blood of the covenant (hinne dam habrit) that YHWH has made with you in accordance with all these words.' This verse is directly quoted by Jesus at the Last Supper in all three Synoptics: 'This is my blood of the covenant' (Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24; Luke 22:20). Jesus's institution of the Eucharist uses the exact covenant-blood formula of Exodus 24:8 to identify his death as the new covenant ratification event. Hebrews 9:18–21 develops the parallel explicitly: the first covenant was not inaugurated without blood.
9: Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders of Israel went up and saw the God of Israel. The covenant ratification culminates in a theophany: the covenant leadership sees YHWH. This is the most extraordinary statement in Exodus — human beings see God and live. They saw the God of Israel — not a symbol, not an angel, but YHWH himself, in some form visible to human eyes. This corporate vision of God anticipates the NT promise: 'they will see his face' (Revelation 22:4); 'we know that when he appears we shall be like him, because we shall see him as he is' (1 John 3:2).
10: 'Under his feet was something like a pavement of sapphire stone, like the very heaven for clearness.' The divine throne's footstool — the sapphire pavement beneath YHWH's feet. Ezekiel's throne-vision (Ezekiel 1:26; Exodus 10:1) uses the same sapphire imagery for the divine throne platform. John's throne-vision (Revelation 4:6) describes 'a sea of glass, like crystal' before the throne. The visual consistency of these divine-throne descriptions across Scripture suggests a consistent heavenly reality partially glimpsed by different covenant witnesses across time.
11: 'He did not lay his hand on the chief men of the people of Israel; they beheld God and ate and drank.' The covenant meal in the divine presence: the elders see God and eat together. This is the most striking theophany in the Hebrew Bible — a corporate meal in the direct divine presence, with no one struck dead. The NT fulfillment is the Lord's Supper: the covenant community eats and drinks in the presence of the risen Christ ('where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them,' Matthew 18:20). The elders' meal before YHWH on Sinai is the OT form of the church's Eucharistic gathering.
12: YHWH tells Moses to come up and receive the stone tablets with the law and commandments. The tablets are described as YHWH's own writing — he has written them for Israel's instruction. The written covenant given to Moses is the foundational document of Israel's covenant life. The NT contrast: 'not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts' (2 Corinthians 3:3). The new covenant's writing medium — the human heart by the Spirit — is the fulfillment of what Jeremiah promised (Exodus 31:33): the law internalized rather than externalized.
13: Moses arose with his assistant Joshua and went up the mountain of God. Joshua alone accompanies Moses partway up; the elders wait at a lower level. The ascending tiers of the mountain approach mirror the ascending holiness of the sanctuary: court, holy place, most holy place. Joshua's presence as assistant at this crucial moment is a foretaste: he will be the one who leads Israel into the land when Moses cannot. The servant who climbs furthest with the mediator inherits the mediator's mission.
14: Moses told the elders: 'Wait for us here until we return — Aaron and Hur are with you.' The delegation of authority in Moses's absence: Aaron and Hur as co-leaders during Moses's forty days. The disaster of the golden calf (Exodus 32) will occur in Moses's absence — demonstrating that delegated authority (Aaron) without the mediator's personal presence is vulnerable to collapse. The NT pattern is different: Christ does not leave his community without his own presence ('I am with you always,' Matthew 28:20) — the Spirit is the Paraclete, the one who stands alongside in the mediator's physical absence.
15: Moses went up and the cloud covered the mountain. The cloud of the divine presence — the same pillar of cloud that led Israel (Exodus 13:21) — now settles on Sinai to cover Moses's ascent. The cloud marks the boundary between the divine and human realms: where the cloud descends, the divine presence is localized. The same cloud overshadows Jesus at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:5) and at the ascension (Acts 1:9). The cloud is the consistent OT symbol of divine presence that the NT identifies as the medium of Christ's own glory-manifestation.
16: The glory of YHWH (kavod YHWH) rested on Sinai for six days, and on the seventh YHWH called to Moses from the cloud. The six-day waiting period and seventh-day call mirrors the creation week — rest on the seventh day, then the new beginning. The pattern of six days of preparation and the seventh day of divine action recurs: Moses waits six days before being called into the divine presence. The seventh day is the day of encounter, of covenant, of new creation. The resurrection on the first/eighth day follows and fulfills this Sabbath-then-new-beginning pattern.
17: The appearance of the glory of YHWH was like a devouring fire (esh okhelet*) on the top of the mountain in the sight of the Israelites. The divine glory seen as fire: both consuming and illuminating, both terrifying and beautiful. This fire-glory appears in the burning bush (Exodus 3:2), the pillar of fire (Exodus 13:21), the Sinai theophany, the cloud filling the tabernacle (Exodus 40:38), and ultimately in the Spirit's tongues of fire at Pentecost (Acts 2:3). The fire-glory of YHWH is the consistent manifestation of his presence, culminating in the Spirit's fire indwelling the community.
18: Moses entered the cloud and went up the mountain — Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights. The forty days and forty nights of Moses on Sinai sets the template for Israel's forty years in the wilderness and for Jesus's forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1–2). The forty-unit period is the time of testing and formation: Moses received the law; Jesus recapitulated Israel's wilderness testing and succeeded where Israel failed. The number forty marks a period of transition between the old condition and the new covenant responsibility that follows.
